subject: Biology
title: Asexual Reproduction in Plants
description: A short lesson to help you apply Asexual Reproduction in Plants.

hook.text: Many plants don't need seeds at all. They can clone themselves from stems, roots, or leaves.

buildup.text: Strawberries send out runners that root and grow into new plants—genetically identical copies.

discovery.text: Potatoes grow from tubers. Each eye on a potato can sprout a whole new plant.

twist.text: A single quaking aspen in Utah—called Pando—is actually 47,000 connected clones sharing one root system.

climax.text: Asexual reproduction is fast and reliable, but it produces no genetic diversity.

punchline.text: Clones spread fast, but one disease can kill them all.

quiz.question: What is Pando?
options: A massive colony of connected aspen clones
